trending_skills.name: NanoClaw
trending_skills.description: Run OpenClaw agents inside containers. Isolation, reproducibility, and easy deployment. Built by a solo dev with Claude Code — Andrej Karpathy noticed it and called OpenClaw 'the orchestration layer for agents.'
trending_skills.why_trending: Karpathy signal-boosted it. Solves the 'my agent broke my system' problem with proper sandboxing. The Register covered it.

trending_skills.name: Deterministic Multi-Agent Pipeline
trending_skills.description: External event bus (Redis Streams/NATS) for agent routing + state machine engine + worker pool control. Turns OpenClaw into a deterministic dev pipeline instead of unpredictable chat.
trending_skills.why_trending: First serious attempt at making multi-agent workflows deterministic. Published on DEV Community with full architecture.

trending_skills.name: Every.to Agent Setup Guide
trending_skills.description: Comprehensive walkthrough for setting up agents that run 24/7. Demos, workflows, and hard-won lessons from an experienced builder. Not a skill — a reference guide.
trending_skills.why_trending: Every.to published it yesterday. Best beginner-to-intermediate guide we've seen. Good for recommending to friends who ask 'what's OpenClaw?'

ai_tweets.headline: Karpathy calls OpenClaw 'the orchestration layer for agents'
ai_tweets.detail: Andrej Karpathy weighed in on the agent framework landscape, positioning OpenClaw and similar 'claws' as the orchestration layer — not the models, not the apps, but the glue between them. This is a significant framing from one of AI's most respected voices.
ai_tweets.takeaway: Validates the thesis that agent orchestration (not models) is the real value layer. If you're building on OpenClaw, you're building on infrastructure, not hype.

ai_tweets.headline: n8n publishes 15 production AI agent examples
ai_tweets.detail: n8n (workflow automation platform) published 15 real-world AI agent patterns: Discord chatbots, educational AI, lead routing, data sync. All with node-based visual workflows.
ai_tweets.takeaway: If you want visual workflow design for agent tasks, n8n is worth exploring. OpenClaw handles the agent; n8n handles the workflow routing.

ai_tweets.headline: Reddit consensus: modular event-driven automations still winning in 2026
ai_tweets.detail: r/AiAutomations thread: 'What's actually working?' Top answers: simple trigger→action patterns, lead routing, AI triage, clean data sync. Complex multi-agent chains still fragile in production.
ai_tweets.takeaway: Keep it simple. Single-agent + good triggers beats multi-agent complexity for most real use cases.

prompt_hack.title: The 'Proactive Agent' Pattern — Stop Waiting to Be Asked
steps: Add to AGENTS.md: 'During heartbeats, check: (1) unread emails, (2) calendar next 24h, (3) any file changes in ~/projects/. If anything needs attention, surface it. If not, reply HEARTBEAT_OK.'
Set heartbeat interval to 30 minutes in your OpenClaw config.
Your agent now checks your world every 30 minutes and only pings you when something actually matters.
prompt_hack.why_it_matters: Most agents sit idle until asked. A proactive agent catches the email you missed, reminds you about the meeting in 2 hours, and flags the git conflict before it blocks you. This is the difference between a tool and a partner.

model_radar.detail: Still expected imminently. China's Two Sessions started today (March 4). Multiple signals point to announcement timing around this event. No official release yet as of publish time.
model_radar.relevance: Keep watching. When it drops, we'll cover benchmarks in the next edition.

model_radar.detail: IntuitionLabs' 2026 enterprise comparison scored Opus 4.6 highest on code and specialized benchmarks (Terminal-Bench). Confirmed as the top choice for agentic coding tasks.
model_radar.relevance: If your agent does heavy coding or file manipulation, Opus 4.6 remains the best option. Worth the premium over Sonnet for complex tasks.

community_build.title: Deterministic Dev Pipeline — Multi-Agent Without the Chaos
community_build.description: A developer built an external orchestration layer for OpenClaw using Redis Streams as an event bus, a state machine engine per project, and a worker spawner with pool control. The result: deterministic multi-agent workflows where each agent has a defined role and handoff protocol. No more unpredictable agent chains.
community_build.how_to_replicate: Core architecture: OpenClaw handles I/O (messaging + execution), Redis Streams handles routing, state machine defines the workflow steps. Full writeup: dev.to/ggondim
